risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Block randomization into two treatment groups with variable block sizes of four to six was performed by an independent epidemiologist using www.randomization.com." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation sequence generated by a randomization website, with block randomization and variable block sizes, which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "To conceal allocation we used sequentially numbered, sealed, opaque envelopes, which were produced before initiation of the study." Comment: This is an explicit allocation-concealment mechanism with all required features (sequentially numbered, sealed, opaque envelopes) and therefore prevents foreknowledge of the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The trial was an open active-comparator study of two different intravenous antiarrhythmic drugs, with no indication that participants or treating personnel were blinded. The methods describe randomization and allocation concealment but do not mention any masking of participants or clinicians, and the interventions (vernakalant vs ibutilide, with different infusion schedules and dosing) were likely readily distinguishable. Comment: Because the main efficacy outcomes were time to sinus rhythm and conversion rate, knowledge of treatment assignment could plausibly influence co-interventions and assessment/management decisions during the procedure, so performance bias is at high risk overall.

Summary: The outcome assessment appears to have been based on continuous ECG monitoring and centrally stored ECG traces for later analysis, rather than subjective clinical judgement. The paper states, "To determine the exact time of conversion to SR, patients received continuous ECG monitoring. These traces were centrally stored in a secure database for later analysis." Comment: The primary efficacy outcome (time to sinus rhythm / conversion to SR) is an objective ECG-derived outcome, so knowledge of allocation is unlikely to have influenced its measurement even though explicit blinding of the outcome assessor is not reported.

Quote: "From 101 randomized patients, 100 (49 in the vernakalant group and 51 in the ibutilide group) received at least one dose of study drug and were included in further analyses (Figure 2). One patient in the ibutilide group received one study drug infusion only because of recurrent nsVT. Another patient in this group weighing 48 kg received weight-adjusted reduced dose according to manufacturer's instructions." Comment: Attrition was essentially complete (1 of 101 randomized patients excluded before treatment because of an eligibility error and replaced), and the remaining randomized participants were analyzed with no sign of differential loss to follow-up or missing outcome data that would plausibly bias the acute efficacy/safety results. The one treated patient who stopped early due to nsVT was still described and contributes to available outcome data rather than unexplained missingness.
